name: Hummus
description: Creamy blended chickpea dip with tahini, lemon, and garlic, drizzled with olive oil.
instructions: Drain and rinse chickpeas; reserve a few for garnish.
Blend tahini and lemon juice in a food processor for 1 minute until thick and creamy.
Add garlic, cumin, and salt; blend for 30 seconds.
Add chickpeas and blend for 2 minutes, scraping sides; drizzle in ice water while blending until very smooth.
Transfer to a bowl, create a well with the back of a spoon, drizzle with olive oil, sprinkle paprika, and top with reserved chickpeas.
tips: Blending tahini and lemon first before adding chickpeas creates the creamiest texture.
Ice water is the secret to ultra-smooth hummus.

name: Falafel
description: Crispy deep-fried chickpea patties seasoned with herbs, cumin, and coriander.
instructions: Soak dried chickpeas overnight in water for at least 12 hours; drain thoroughly.
Pulse chickpeas, onion, parsley, cilantro, and garlic in a food processor until coarsely ground — do not puree.
Mix in cumin, coriander, salt, pepper, and baking powder; refrigerate for 1 hour.
Shape into small patties or balls about 3cm wide.
Deep-fry in oil at 175C for 3-4 minutes until deep golden brown; drain on paper towels and serve with tahini sauce.
tips: You must use dried chickpeas soaked overnight — canned chickpeas will fall apart when fried.
Do not over-process the mixture; it should be coarse and hold together when pressed.

name: Shawarma Chicken
description: Warm-spiced chicken thighs roasted until caramelized, served with garlic sauce and pickles.
instructions: Mix yogurt, all spices, minced garlic, lemon juice, olive oil, and salt into a marinade.
Coat chicken thighs thoroughly and marinate for at least 2 hours or overnight.
Roast on a baking sheet at 220C for 25-30 minutes until edges are deeply caramelized.
Rest for 5 minutes, then slice thinly against the grain.
Serve in warm pita with garlic sauce, pickled turnips, and fresh vegetables.
tips: Chicken thighs stay juicier than breast for shawarma — the higher fat content bastes the meat as it cooks.
Broil for the last 2 minutes for extra charred edges.

name: Tabbouleh
description: Fresh parsley and bulgur wheat salad with tomatoes, mint, lemon, and olive oil.
instructions: Soak bulgur in boiling water for 10 minutes; drain thoroughly and squeeze out excess moisture.
Finely chop parsley, mint, tomatoes, and green onions — parsley should be the dominant ingredient.
Combine bulgur with all chopped vegetables and herbs.
Dress with lemon juice, olive oil, salt, and pepper; toss well.
Let sit 10 minutes before serving to allow flavors to meld.
tips: Authentic tabbouleh is mostly parsley with a small amount of bulgur — not the other way around.
Dry the bulgur well after soaking to prevent a soggy salad.

name: Fattoush
description: Crunchy Lebanese bread salad with mixed greens, radishes, and tangy sumac dressing.
instructions: Tear pita into pieces, toast in oven at 200C for 8 minutes until crispy and golden.
Chop lettuce, dice cucumber and tomatoes, slice radishes and green onions.
Whisk olive oil, lemon juice, sumac, pomegranate molasses, and salt into a dressing.
Combine all vegetables in a bowl, toss with dressing.
Add crispy pita just before serving so it stays crunchy.
tips: Add the pita at the very last moment — it absorbs moisture quickly and loses its crunch.
Sumac is essential and not optional; it gives fattoush its signature tangy flavor.

name: Baba Ganoush
description: Smoky roasted eggplant dip blended with tahini, lemon, and garlic.
instructions: Char eggplants directly over a gas flame or under a broiler, turning frequently, until skin is blackened and flesh is completely soft.
Let cool, then peel off the charred skin; drain excess liquid from the flesh.
Mash eggplant flesh with a fork — do not use a food processor for authentic texture.
Mix in tahini, lemon juice, minced garlic, cumin, and salt.
Drizzle with olive oil, garnish with parsley, and serve with warm pita.
tips: The smokiness comes from charring directly on the flame — an oven will not give the same flavor.
Draining the liquid from the cooked eggplant prevents a watery dip.

name: Shakshuka
description: Eggs poached in a spiced tomato and pepper sauce with cumin and paprika.
instructions: Heat olive oil in a deep skillet, sauté diced onion and bell pepper until soft.
Add minced garlic, cumin, paprika, and chili flakes; cook for 1 minute until fragrant.
Pour in canned tomatoes, break them up, season with salt, and simmer for 10 minutes until thickened.
Make 4 wells in the sauce and crack an egg into each well.
Cover and cook on low heat for 6-8 minutes until egg whites are set but yolks are still runny; top with crumbled feta and cilantro.
tips: Cover the pan to trap steam — this cooks the egg whites from the top without overcooking the yolks.
Serve straight from the skillet with crusty bread for dipping.

name: Lamb Kofta
description: Spiced ground lamb skewers grilled until charred and served with yogurt sauce.
instructions: Grate the onion and squeeze out excess moisture.
Mix ground lamb with grated onion, chopped parsley, minced garlic, and all spices; knead until well combined.
Divide into 8 portions and shape each around a metal skewer in an elongated oval.
Grill or broil for 4-5 minutes per side until charred outside and cooked through.
Serve with warm pita, yogurt sauce, and a simple salad.
tips: Squeezing the moisture from grated onion prevents the kofta from falling apart on the grill.
Metal skewers conduct heat and cook the kofta from the inside as well.

name: Moussaka
description: Layered casserole of spiced lamb, eggplant, and creamy bechamel sauce baked golden.
instructions: Slice eggplant into 1cm rounds, salt and let drain for 30 minutes; pat dry and brush with olive oil, then grill or bake at 200C until golden.
Brown ground lamb with diced onion and garlic; add tomatoes, cinnamon, allspice, and salt; simmer 15 minutes.
Make bechamel: melt butter, stir in flour, slowly whisk in milk until thick; remove from heat and stir in egg yolk and nutmeg.
Layer eggplant slices, then meat sauce, then more eggplant in a baking dish; pour bechamel over the top.
Bake at 180C for 40 minutes until golden and bubbly; let rest 15 minutes before cutting.
tips: Salting and draining the eggplant removes bitterness and excess moisture.
Resting after baking lets the layers set so slices hold together.

name: Stuffed Grape Leaves
description: Tender grape leaves filled with herbed rice, pine nuts, and lemon, served cold.
instructions: Rinse grape leaves and blanch in boiling water for 2 minutes; drain and pat dry.
Mix uncooked rice with finely diced onion, chopped herbs, pine nuts, half the lemon juice, 2 tbsp olive oil, allspice, cinnamon, and salt.
Place a tablespoon of filling at the base of each leaf, fold sides in, and roll tightly.
Arrange rolls seam-side down in a pot lined with extra grape leaves; drizzle with remaining olive oil and lemon juice.
Add water, place a plate on top to weigh them down, cover, and simmer on low for 45 minutes; serve cold or at room temperature.
tips: The plate on top prevents the rolls from unraveling during cooking.
These taste even better the next day after the flavors have melded.

name: Labneh
description: Thick strained yogurt spread drizzled with olive oil and served with warm pita.
instructions: Mix yogurt with salt and place in a cheesecloth-lined strainer set over a bowl.
Refrigerate and strain for at least 12 hours or overnight until thick and creamy.
Transfer the thick labneh to a serving plate and spread into a shallow layer.
Drizzle generously with olive oil and sprinkle with za'atar and torn mint leaves.
Serve with warm pita bread for dipping.
tips: The longer you strain, the thicker it gets — 24 hours gives a cream cheese consistency.
Use full-fat yogurt for the richest, creamiest results.

name: Musakhan
description: Palestinian sumac chicken baked on caramelized onion flatbread with pine nuts.
instructions: Season chicken with 1 tbsp sumac, allspice, cinnamon, salt, and 2 tbsp olive oil; roast at 200C for 30 minutes until golden.
Meanwhile, slice onions thinly and cook in remaining olive oil over medium-low heat for 20 minutes until deeply caramelized.
Stir remaining sumac into the caramelized onions and cook 2 more minutes.
Spread the sumac onions over the flatbread, place roasted chicken on top, and return to oven for 10 minutes.
Toast pine nuts in a dry pan until golden and scatter over the dish before serving.
tips: Low and slow caramelization of the onions is the heart of this dish — do not rush it.
Sumac should be fresh and vibrant purple-red for the best flavor.

name: Kabsa
description: Saudi Arabian spiced rice with tender chicken, tomatoes, and aromatic whole spices.
instructions: Brown chicken pieces in olive oil on all sides; remove and set aside.
In the same pot, sauté diced onion until golden, add garlic, whole spices, and ground spices; cook 1 minute.
Add chopped tomatoes and tomato paste; cook 5 minutes until reduced.
Wash and drain rice, add to the pot with water and salt; nestle chicken pieces on top.
Bring to a boil, reduce to low, cover tightly, and cook for 20-25 minutes until rice is fluffy and chicken is cooked through.
tips: Do not stir the rice once it is layered — let it steam undisturbed for the best texture.
Soaking the rice for 20 minutes before cooking shortens the cook time and gives fluffier grains.

name: Manakish
description: Lebanese flatbread topped with za'atar and olive oil, baked until golden and fragrant.
instructions: Mix flour, yeast, sugar, and salt; add olive oil and warm water; knead into a smooth dough for 5 minutes.
Cover and let rise in a warm place for 30 minutes until doubled.
Divide dough into 4 balls, roll each into a thin round about 20cm wide.
Mix za'atar with olive oil into a paste and spread generously over each round.
Bake at 220C on a preheated baking sheet for 8-10 minutes until edges are golden and crispy.
tips: A very hot oven and preheated baking sheet mimic a traditional bread oven.
The dough can be made the night before and refrigerated for a quicker morning bake.

name: Kibbeh
description: Crispy bulgur shells stuffed with spiced ground lamb, onions, and toasted pine nuts.
instructions: Soak bulgur in water for 30 minutes; drain and squeeze out excess moisture.
Make the shell: blend soaked bulgur with lean ground lamb, half the grated onion, allspice, and salt until a smooth paste forms.
Make the filling: sauté remaining diced onion, add ground lamb, pine nuts, cinnamon, cumin, and pepper; cook until browned.
Shape kibbeh: take a golf-ball-sized portion of the shell mixture, hollow it out, fill with a spoonful of filling, and seal into a torpedo shape.
Deep-fry at 175C for 4-5 minutes until deep golden and crispy; drain on paper towels.
tips: Keep your hands wet while shaping to prevent the bulgur mixture from sticking.
The shell mixture must be very smooth — blend it twice if needed.

name: Scrambled Eggs
description: Soft, creamy scrambled eggs cooked low and slow with butter and a touch of cream.
instructions: Crack eggs into a bowl and whisk with cream, salt, and pepper until uniform.
Melt butter in a non-stick pan over low heat.
Pour in eggs and stir slowly and continuously with a spatula, pulling curds from the edges to the center.
Remove from heat while eggs are still slightly wet — they will continue cooking from residual heat.
Serve immediately, garnished with chopped chives.
tips: Low heat and patience are the keys — high heat makes eggs rubbery.
Remove from heat just before they look done; carryover cooking finishes them perfectly.

name: Pancakes
description: Fluffy buttermilk pancakes stacked high and served with maple syrup and butter.
instructions: Mix flour, baking powder, baking soda, sugar, and salt in a bowl.
Whisk buttermilk, egg, and melted butter in a separate bowl.
Fold wet ingredients into dry until just combined — lumps are fine.
Heat a non-stick pan or griddle over medium heat, pour 1/4 cup batter per pancake.
Cook until bubbles form and edges set, flip once, cook 1-2 more minutes; serve stacked with butter and maple syrup.
tips: Do not over-mix the batter — lumps are perfectly fine and lead to fluffier pancakes.
Let the batter rest 5 minutes before cooking for the best rise.

name: French Toast
description: Thick bread slices soaked in vanilla egg custard, pan-fried golden and dusted with cinnamon sugar.
instructions: Whisk eggs, milk, vanilla, cinnamon, and sugar into a smooth custard.
Dip each bread slice into the custard, letting it soak for 10 seconds per side.
Melt butter in a pan over medium heat and cook the soaked slices for 2-3 minutes per side until golden.
Dust with powdered sugar and drizzle with maple syrup.
Serve immediately with fresh berries if desired.
tips: Use day-old bread or thick brioche — it absorbs the custard without falling apart.
Medium heat is key; too high and the outside burns before the inside cooks.

name: Omelette
description: Classic folded omelette with cheese, mushrooms, and fresh herbs cooked in butter.
instructions: Whisk eggs with milk, salt, and pepper until well combined.
Sauté sliced mushrooms in half the butter until golden; set aside.
Melt remaining butter in a non-stick pan over medium heat, pour in eggs, and swirl to coat the pan.
When edges set, gently push cooked egg toward center and tilt pan to let raw egg flow to the edges.
Add cheese and mushrooms to one half, fold over, and slide onto a plate; garnish with chives.
tips: A non-stick pan and medium heat are essential for a smooth, unbroken omelette.
Fill the omelette while the center is still slightly wet — it finishes cooking from the residual heat.

name: Grilled Cheese Sandwich
description: Golden crispy buttered bread with melted gooey cheese inside.
instructions: Butter the outside of both bread slices.
Spread a thin layer of Dijon mustard on the inside of one slice.
Layer cheese between the slices with buttered sides facing out.
Cook in a pan over medium-low heat for 3-4 minutes per side until bread is deeply golden and cheese is fully melted.
Cut diagonally and serve hot.
tips: Medium-low heat gives the cheese time to melt before the bread burns.
A mix of cheddar and gruyere gives the best stretch and flavor.

name: Tomato Soup
description: Velvety smooth roasted tomato soup finished with cream and fresh basil.
instructions: Sauté diced onion and garlic in olive oil until soft and translucent.
Add canned tomatoes with their juice, vegetable broth, sugar, salt, and pepper; bring to a simmer.
Cook for 20 minutes to concentrate the flavors.
Blend until completely smooth using an immersion blender or regular blender.
Stir in cream, taste and adjust seasoning, and serve topped with torn basil leaves.
tips: A pinch of sugar balances the acidity of the tomatoes without making it sweet.
For a dairy-free version, substitute coconut cream for the heavy cream.

name: Caesar Salad
description: Crisp romaine lettuce tossed with creamy Caesar dressing, croutons, and parmesan.
instructions: Cut bread into cubes, toss with 1 tbsp olive oil and a pinch of salt; bake at 200C for 8 minutes until golden croutons.
Make dressing: whisk egg yolk with anchovy paste, mustard, minced garlic, lemon juice, and Worcestershire; slowly drizzle in remaining olive oil while whisking.
Wash and dry romaine, tear into bite-sized pieces.
Toss lettuce with dressing until evenly coated.
Top with croutons and shaved parmesan; serve immediately.
tips: The dressing emulsifies best at room temperature — take the egg out ahead of time.
Anchovy paste is the backbone of Caesar dressing; do not skip it.

name: Garlic Bread
description: Crusty baguette slathered with garlic herb butter and toasted until golden.
instructions: Mix softened butter with minced garlic, chopped parsley, and salt until well combined.
Slice baguette in half lengthwise.
Spread garlic butter generously over the cut sides.
Place on a baking sheet and bake at 200C for 10-12 minutes until edges are golden and crispy.
Cut into portions and serve warm.
tips: Wrap in foil for the first 8 minutes for soft bread, or bake open for maximum crispiness.
Use room temperature butter for easier mixing and spreading.

name: Mashed Potatoes
description: Creamy buttery mashed potatoes whipped until smooth and fluffy.
instructions: Peel potatoes, cut into even chunks, and place in cold salted water.
Bring to a boil and cook for 15-18 minutes until fork-tender.
Drain thoroughly and return to the pot on low heat for 1 minute to evaporate excess moisture.
Mash with a potato masher or ricer; fold in butter, warm milk, and cream.
Season with salt and white pepper; serve immediately.
tips: Starting in cold water ensures the potatoes cook evenly from the outside in.
Never use a food processor or blender — it turns potatoes gluey.

name: Pasta Aglio e Olio
description: Spaghetti tossed in golden garlic olive oil with chili flakes and fresh parsley.
instructions: Cook spaghetti in generously salted boiling water until al dente; reserve 1 cup pasta water before draining.
While pasta cooks, slowly heat olive oil with thinly sliced garlic over medium-low heat until garlic is golden — not brown.
Add chili flakes and cook 30 seconds more.
Toss drained pasta into the garlic oil with a splash of pasta water; toss vigorously to emulsify.
Serve topped with chopped parsley and grated parmesan.
tips: Slice garlic thin and cook low and slow — burnt garlic is bitter and ruins the dish.
Pasta water is essential for creating a silky sauce that clings to the noodles.

name: Banana Smoothie
description: Creamy blended banana smoothie with yogurt, honey, and a hint of cinnamon.
instructions: Peel bananas and break into chunks.
Add banana, yogurt, milk, honey, cinnamon, and ice cubes to a blender.
Blend on high for 30-60 seconds until completely smooth and frothy.
Pour into glasses and serve immediately.
tips: Frozen banana slices make a thicker, colder smoothie without diluting with too much ice.
Use ripe bananas with brown spots for the sweetest, most flavorful smoothie.

name: Overnight Oats
description: Rolled oats soaked overnight in milk with chia seeds, topped with fruit and honey.
instructions: Combine oats, milk, chia seeds, honey, and vanilla in a jar.
Stir well, cover, and refrigerate overnight for at least 6 hours.
In the morning, stir and top with sliced banana and blueberries.
Add a splash of milk if the consistency is too thick.
tips: Use a mason jar for easy grab-and-go breakfast.
Any milk works — dairy, almond, or oat milk all produce great results.

name: Avocado Toast
description: Mashed avocado on toasted sourdough with lemon, chili flakes, and a poached egg.
instructions: Toast bread until golden and crispy.
Halve avocado, scoop flesh, and mash with lemon juice, salt, and pepper.
Spread mashed avocado thickly on toast.
Poach an egg in simmering water with a splash of vinegar for 3 minutes; place on top.
Sprinkle chili flakes and serve immediately.
tips: Use a ripe avocado — it should give slightly when pressed.
A splash of vinegar in the poaching water helps the egg white hold together.

name: Quesadilla
description: Crispy flour tortilla filled with melted cheese, peppers, and onions.
instructions: Sauté diced bell pepper, onion, and jalapeño until soft.
Place one tortilla in a dry pan, sprinkle half the cheese, add the cooked vegetables, then remaining cheese and second tortilla.
Cook on medium heat for 2-3 minutes until bottom is golden and crispy.
Flip carefully and cook the other side until golden.
Cut into wedges and serve with sour cream and salsa.
tips: Low-medium heat gives the cheese time to melt before the tortilla burns.
Press down gently while cooking for even crisping.

name: Bruschetta
description: Toasted crusty bread topped with diced tomatoes, fresh basil, garlic, and olive oil.
instructions: Dice tomatoes, toss with torn basil, 2 tbsp olive oil, balsamic vinegar, salt, and pepper; let sit 10 minutes.
Slice bread into 1cm thick rounds and brush with remaining olive oil.
Toast bread under a broiler or on a grill for 1-2 minutes per side until golden.
Rub one side of each toast with a cut garlic clove while still warm.
Spoon the tomato mixture on top and serve immediately.
tips: Use the ripest tomatoes you can find — this dish lives or dies by tomato quality.
Rubbing warm toast with raw garlic infuses just enough flavor without being overwhelming.

name: Fruit Salad
description: Fresh mixed seasonal fruits tossed in a light honey-lime dressing with mint.
instructions: Wash all fruit; hull and halve strawberries, peel and dice mango and kiwi, halve grapes, slice banana.
Combine all prepared fruit in a large bowl.
Whisk honey and lime juice together until dissolved.
Drizzle the honey-lime dressing over the fruit and toss gently.
Garnish with torn mint leaves and serve chilled.
tips: Add banana just before serving to prevent browning.
Use whatever seasonal fruits are at their best — the recipe is flexible.
